common_name: Royal Burgundy Bush Bean (Purple Pod Snap Bean That Turns Green When Cooked)

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost when soil has warmed above 60°F in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; the purple pod color disappears when Royal Burgundy is blanched or cooked — pods turn standard green, which some gardeners find surprising.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1–2 inches deep after the last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun, thinning to 4–6 inches apart; Royal Burgundy is a bush snap bean producing vivid deep-purple pods that are immediately striking in the garden and make harvesting easy — the bright-purple color stands out against green foliage so ripe pods are much easier to spot than the green pods of standard snap beans. The purple color comes from anthocyanin pigments that disappear completely when the beans are cooked — pods turn a standard snap-bean green when blanched or boiled, which is useful to know so the color loss is not mistaken for poor quality. The flavor is excellent: full, classic snap bean flavor with slightly more robust character than some modern hybrids. Royal Burgundy is notably cold-soil tolerant for a snap bean — germinating reliably in soil temperatures down to 60°F (versus the 65°F+ preferred by most snap beans), making it a good choice for early spring and fall plantings in zone 9 where soil temperatures can be borderline for bean germination. The bush habit (18–24 inches, no staking required) and reliable early productivity (50–60 days) make Royal Burgundy one of the more practical ornamental-vegetables for Gulf South kitchen gardens, combining harvest efficiency with decorative garden impact.

common_name: Fish Pepper (African American Heirloom Striped Hot Pepper with Variegated Foliage)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun; the spectacular variegated foliage and striped fruit coloring of Fish Pepper make it equally valuable as an ornamental in herb and pepper gardens.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Fish Pepper is an African American heirloom pepper with a unique history — the variety was maintained in the Black community of Baltimore and the Chesapeake Bay region through the 19th and early 20th centuries, where the unripe green-striped fruits were used to season fish and shellfish dishes without the red coloring that would be visible in pale-sauced seafood preparations. Rediscovered by artist and food historian William Woys Weaver through Seed Savers Exchange, Fish Pepper is now recognized as one of the most historically significant African American food heritage vegetables. The ornamental impact is extraordinary: the plant produces dramatically variegated, cream-and-dark-green streaked foliage unlike any other pepper variety, and the immature fruits are striped in cream and pale green, ripening through orange-and-cream striped stages to solid deep red at full maturity. The range of stripe patterns and color stages at different ripeness levels creates a constantly changing visual display. The heat level is medium-hot (comparable to cayenne, 30,000–50,000 SHU) — appropriate for seasoning fish sauces, seafood dishes, and hot sauces. The ornamental quality of both foliage and fruit at all stages makes Fish Pepper an outstanding choice for kitchen garden beds and container growing on patios.

common_name: Lipstick Sweet Pepper (AAS-Winning Thick-Walled Pimento-Type Red Sweet Pepper)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Lipstick produces fruits earlier than most large bell peppers — the shorter maturity window is valuable in zone 9 where spring heat can shorten the productive window for sweet peppers.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Lipstick is an All-America Selections-winning pimento-type sweet pepper producing small to medium (2–3 inch), thick-walled, blocky, 2-lobed red fruits with exceptional sweetness and a characteristic heart-shaped profile that gives the variety its name. The pimento type is fundamentally different from standard bell peppers: thicker walls, lower water content, higher sugar concentration, and a richer, sweeter flavor that makes pimento-type peppers superior for fresh eating, roasting, and cheese stuffing. The walls of Lipstick peppers are noticeably thicker and meatier relative to the cavity size than large bell types — the high flesh-to-cavity ratio creates the characteristic pimento sweetness and dense texture. Lipstick matures from green to deep red at 70–80 days from transplant — faster than most large bell peppers (75–90 days), an important advantage in zone 9 where spring heat can end the productive bell pepper window before late-maturing varieties ripen. The compact, productive plant sets fruit heavily and the small-to-medium fruit size means the plant is less challenged by the heat load of maturing large individual fruits. Outstanding for roasting, for eating fresh off the plant, and for the classic Southern pimento cheese preparation.

common_name: Caribbean Red Habanero (Extremely Hot Red Habanero Type with Fruity Heat)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Capsicum chinense species require warmer germination temperatures (80–90°F) and longer germination times than C. annuum — use a heat mat for reliable, fast germination.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Caribbean Red is a habanero-type pepper (Capsicum chinense) producing wrinkled, lantern-shaped fruits that ripen from green to bright scarlet-red — one of the hottest commercially available habanero types, typically measuring 400,000–500,000 Scoville heat units compared to the 100,000–350,000 SHU of standard orange habaneros, making it roughly 2–4 times hotter than standard habanero by Scoville measurement. The heat is the intense, building, lingering habanero burn style — not the immediate sharp heat of cayenne — accompanied by the same characteristic fruity, tropical, citrus-floral aroma that makes habanero-type peppers uniquely valuable in hot sauces, salsas, Caribbean jerk seasonings, and mango-habanero condiments. The red color produces a visually different (and often considered more attractive) hot sauce than the orange of standard habanero, making Caribbean Red popular with home hot sauce makers. The large, productive plants grow well in zone 9's long warm season, often continuing to fruit into November after a spring transplant. In zone 9b and warmer, the plants may overwinter and become large perennial pepper shrubs producing for multiple seasons.

common_name: Orange King Bougainvillea (Warm Copper-Orange Bougainvillea Cultivar)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost in well-drained, lean to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Orange King blooms most prolifically under slight water stress — allow the soil to dry somewhat between waterings and limit nitrogen fertilizer to promote flowering over vegetative growth.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to average soil in full sun; Orange King is a bougainvillea cultivar producing bracts in warm copper-orange to apricot-orange tones — a distinctly different color character from the hot pinks and magentas of Barbara Karst or the scarlet-red of San Diego Red, offering instead a warmer, earthier color tone that reads more tropical and less vivid in the landscape. The orange-copper bracts combine particularly well with terracotta container materials, yellow walls, and companion plants in warm orange, yellow, and bronze tones. Orange King blooms in the same seasonal pattern as other bougainvillea cultivars in zone 9 — most heavily in spring and fall as temperatures moderate, with reduced bloom during peak summer heat and winter cold. The bract color in Orange King tends to fade to salmon-pink and peachy tones as the bracts age on the plant, creating a natural color progression from rich copper-orange at peak freshness to faded salmon-apricot as the bloom matures — a pleasing multi-tone effect during extended bloom periods. The growth rate and vigor are typical of bougainvillea: rapid once established in zone 9 conditions, capable of covering substantial wall or fence areas. The copper-orange color has become popular in Spanish Colonial, Mediterranean, and Southwestern landscape styles where the warm tones harmonize with stucco walls and warm architectural palettes.
